structure.title: RMT 2022 -2023

nodes.title: AIM: To study the different microwave components.
nodes.text:
RMT  2022 -2023    
 
D.Y.  Patil  College  of Engineering,  Akurdi,  Pune  – 44 
 
Department  of Electronics  and Telecommunication  Engineering  
 
 
Name  of student:  Anjali Kumari  PRN  No.:72017900K  
Class  & Division:  BE-B Roll No.: BEB224  
 
 
Experiment  No.: 
 
Date  of Performance:  
 
AIM:  To study  the different microwave  components.  
 
APPARATUS:  E-plane  Tee, H-plane  Tee, E-H plane  Tee, Directional  coupler,  Isolator,  
Attenuator  and Circulator.  
 
THEORY:  
 
Introduction  To Microwave  Communications  
Microwaves are electromagnetic waves (E.M. waves) having wavelength in the micron range.  
Though microwave frequencies refer to those from 1GHz to 106GHz but generally used for those  
wavelengths measured in centimeters, roughly from 10cm to 1cm(3 to 30 GHz)  and the waves  
having  wavelengths  less than 1cm corresponds  to higher  frequencies(>30  GHz)  are called  
millimeter  waves (mm waves).  
Microwave  Frequencies  
Relationship  between  the frequency  (f) and the wavelength  ( λ) of an E.M. wave  is λ 
f=c 
Where  c is velocity  of electromagnetic  radiation,  usually  called the speed  of light.  
IEEE  Microwave  Frequency  Band:  
Designation  Frequency  range  in GHz  
HF 0.003 to 0.03  
VHF  0.03 to 0.3  
UHF  0.3 to 1.0  
L-Band  1.0 to 2.0  
S-Band  2.0 to 4.0  
C-Band  4.0 to 8.0  
X-Band  8.0to 12.0  
Ku-Band  12.0 to 27.0
nodes.summary: The partial document is a laboratory experiment record from the Department of Electronics and Telecommunication Engineering at D.Y. Patil College of Engineering, Akurdi, Pune. It includes details of a student, Anjali Kumari, and outlines an experiment aimed at studying different microwave components. The document lists the apparatus required, such as E-plane Tee, H-plane Tee, E-H plane Tee, directional coupler, isolator, attenuator, and circulator. It provides an introduction to microwave communications, explaining microwaves as electromagnetic waves with specific frequency and wavelength ranges. The relationship between frequency and wavelength is described, and the IEEE microwave frequency bands are detailed, covering ranges from HF (0.003 GHz) to Ku-Band (12.0 to 27.0 GHz).

nodes.title: IEEE Microwave Frequency Band:
nodes.text:
RMT  2022 -2023    
 
D.Y.  Patil  College  of Engineering,  Akurdi,  Pune  – 44 
 
Department  of Electronics  and Telecommunication  Engineering  
 
 
Name  of student:  Anjali Kumari  PRN  No.:72017900K  
Class  & Division:  BE-B Roll No.: BEB224  
 
 
Experiment  No.: 
 
Date  of Performance:  
 
AIM:  To study  the different microwave  components.  
 
APPARATUS:  E-plane  Tee, H-plane  Tee, E-H plane  Tee, Directional  coupler,  Isolator,  
Attenuator  and Circulator.  
 
THEORY:  
 
Introduction  To Microwave  Communications  
Microwaves are electromagnetic waves (E.M. waves) having wavelength in the micron range.  
Though microwave frequencies refer to those from 1GHz to 106GHz but generally used for those  
wavelengths measured in centimeters, roughly from 10cm to 1cm(3 to 30 GHz)  and the waves  
having  wavelengths  less than 1cm corresponds  to higher  frequencies(>30  GHz)  are called  
millimeter  waves (mm waves).  
Microwave  Frequencies  
Relationship  between  the frequency  (f) and the wavelength  ( λ) of an E.M. wave  is λ 
f=c 
Where  c is velocity  of electromagnetic  radiation,  usually  called the speed  of light.  
IEEE  Microwave  Frequency  Band:  
Designation  Frequency  range  in GHz  
HF 0.003 to 0.03  
VHF  0.03 to 0.3  
UHF  0.3 to 1.0  
L-Band  1.0 to 2.0  
S-Band  2.0 to 4.0  
C-Band  4.0 to 8.0  
X-Band  8.0to 12.0  
Ku-Band  12.0 to 27.0  
RMT  2022 -2023   K- Band  18.0 to 27.0  
Ka-Band  27.0 to 40.0  
Millimeter  40.0 to 300  
Sub-millimeter  300 and above.  
Characteristic  Features of  Microwave  And Applications  
Most  of the applications  of microwave  arise  from  the characteristic  features  and their 
advantages.  First important  characteristic  is that the microwave  is highly  directive  which  makes  
it possible  for microwave  communi cation  used for telephone  networks,  radio  broadcasting  and 
television systems. Like any other energy, microwave energy has a heating effect, it is used in  
microwave oven for home cooking, drying machine, drying inks, and in food processing  
industries.  
Microwaves are capable of energetically interacting with matter and so used in microwave  
spectroscopy  for structural  analysis.  Apart  from  scientific  research  the absorption  of microwave  
by molecular resonance is well suited for various industrial measureme nts like control of  
pollution  by checking  the concentration  of different  gases  from an exhaust chimney.  
Microwave  Systems:  
Microwave system normally consists of several microwave components including the source  
and the load being connected to each other by  waveguide or co -axial or transmission line  
system.  All these  components  must  be built with low standing  wave  ratios,  low attenuator,  lower  
insertion  losses  and character  to achieve  the desired  transmission  of microwave  signal.  
Following  are the different microwave  components.  
1. E-plane  Tee 
A   rectangular slot is cut along the broader dimension of a long waveguide and a  
side arm is attached as shown in fig. Port 1 and Port 2 are the collinear arms and Port 3 is the  
E-arm. 
When  TE10 mode  is made  to propagate  into Port 3 the two outputs  at Port 1 and 2 will have 
a phase shift of 1800.Since the electric field lines change their direction when they come out  
of Port 1 and Port 2, it is called an E -plane Tee. E -plane Tee is a voltage or series junction  
symmetrical  about  the central  arm. Hence  any signals  that is to be split or any two signals  that 
are to be  combined will be  fed from the E  arm. 
 
 
 
 
 
Figure  1: (a) Schematic  of E-plane  Tee (b) 
Construction  (c) Electric field  lines with  180°  
phase  shift
nodes.summary: The partial document is a laboratory experiment report from the Department of Electronics and Telecommunication Engineering at D.Y. Patil College of Engineering, Akurdi, Pune. It includes details such as the student's name, class, roll number, and the aim of the experiment, which is to study different microwave components. The document provides an introduction to microwave communications, explaining the nature of microwaves, their frequency ranges, and the IEEE microwave frequency bands. It highlights the characteristic features and applications of microwaves, such as their directive nature, use in communication systems, heating applications, spectroscopy, and industrial measurements. The document also describes microwave systems and components, focusing on the E-plane Tee, its structure, functionality, and phase shift characteristics, supported by a schematic diagram.

nodes.title: Characteristic Features of Microwave And Applications
nodes.text:
RMT  2022 -2023   K- Band  18.0 to 27.0  
Ka-Band  27.0 to 40.0  
Millimeter  40.0 to 300  
Sub-millimeter  300 and above.  
Characteristic  Features of  Microwave  And Applications  
Most  of the applications  of microwave  arise  from  the characteristic  features  and their 
advantages.  First important  characteristic  is that the microwave  is highly  directive  which  makes  
it possible  for microwave  communi cation  used for telephone  networks,  radio  broadcasting  and 
television systems. Like any other energy, microwave energy has a heating effect, it is used in

industries.

spectroscopy  for structural  analysis.  Apart  from  scientific  research  the absorption  of microwave  
by molecular resonance is well suited for various industrial measureme nts like control of  
pollution  by checking  the concentration  of different  gases  from an exhaust chimney.

nodes.summary: The partial document provides an overview of microwave frequency bands (K-Band, Ka-Band, Millimeter, and Sub-millimeter) and their characteristic features and applications. It highlights the directive nature of microwaves, enabling their use in communication systems like telephone networks, radio broadcasting, and television. The heating effect of microwaves is discussed, with applications in microwave ovens, drying machines, and food processing industries. The document also mentions the use of microwaves in spectroscopy for structural analysis and industrial measurements, such as pollution control by monitoring gas concentrations. Additionally, it describes the components of a microwave system, emphasizing the importance of low standing wave ratios, low attenuation, and low insertion losses for effective signal transmission. The E-plane Tee is introduced as a microwave component, explaining its structure, functionality, and phase-shifting properties.

nodes.title: Microwave Systems:
E-plane Tee
nodes.text:
RMT  2022 -2023   K- Band  18.0 to 27.0  
Ka-Band  27.0 to 40.0  
Millimeter  40.0 to 300  
Sub-millimeter  300 and above.  
Characteristic  Features of  Microwave  And Applications  
Most  of the applications  of microwave  arise  from  the characteristic  features  and their 
advantages.  First important  characteristic  is that the microwave  is highly  directive  which  makes  
it possible  for microwave  communi cation  used for telephone  networks,  radio  broadcasting  and 
television systems. Like any other energy, microwave energy has a heating effect, it is used in  
microwave oven for home cooking, drying machine, drying inks, and in food processing  
industries.  
Microwaves are capable of energetically interacting with matter and so used in microwave  
spectroscopy  for structural  analysis.  Apart  from  scientific  research  the absorption  of microwave  
by molecular resonance is well suited for various industrial measureme nts like control of  
pollution  by checking  the concentration  of different  gases  from an exhaust chimney.  
Microwave  Systems:  
Microwave system normally consists of several microwave components including the source  
and the load being connected to each other by  waveguide or co -axial or transmission line  
system.  All these  components  must  be built with low standing  wave  ratios,  low attenuator,  lower  
insertion  losses  and character  to achieve  the desired  transmission  of microwave  signal.  
Following  are the different microwave  components.  
1. E-plane  Tee 
A   rectangular slot is cut along the broader dimension of a long waveguide and a  
side arm is attached as shown in fig. Port 1 and Port 2 are the collinear arms and Port 3 is the  
E-arm. 
When  TE10 mode  is made  to propagate  into Port 3 the two outputs  at Port 1 and 2 will have 
a phase shift of 1800.Since the electric field lines change their direction when they come out  
of Port 1 and Port 2, it is called an E -plane Tee. E -plane Tee is a voltage or series junction  
symmetrical  about  the central  arm. Hence  any signals  that is to be split or any two signals  that 
are to be  combined will be  fed from the E  arm. 
 
 
 
 
 
Figure  1: (a) Schematic  of E-plane  Tee (b) 
Construction  (c) Electric field  lines with  180°  
phase  shift  
RMT  2022 -2023   2. H-plane  Tee 
A H-plane  Tee junction  is formed  by cutting  a rectangular  slot along  the width  of a 
main  waveguide  and attaching  another  waveguide  the side arm called  the H-arm as  shown  
in figure  2. The port 1 and port 2 of the main  waveguide  are called  collinear  ports  and port 
3 is the  H-arm or  side arm. 
H-plane  Tee is so called  because  the axis of the side arm is parallel  to the planes  of 
the main transmission line. As all three arms of H -plane Tee lie in the plane of magnetic  
field, the magnetic field divides itself into the arms. Therefore this is also called a current  
junction.  
 
 
Figure  2: (a) Schematic  details  and (b) constru ction  of H-plane  Tee Junction  
 
3. E-H plane  Tee 
A H-plane Tee junction is formed by cutting a rectangular slot along the width and  
breadth  of a long waveguide  and side arms  are attached  as shown  in figure  3. Ports  1 and 2 are 
collinear  arms, port 3 is the  H-arm, and port 4 is the E -arm. 
Such a device became necessary because of the difficulty of obtaining a completely  
matched three port Tee junction. This four port hybrid Tee junction combines the power dividing  
properties of both H -plane Tee and E -plane Tee and has the advantage of being completely  
matched  at all its ports.  
Figure  3: E-H Plane  (a) Schematic  (b) Construction  
 
 
4. Directional  Coupler
nodes.summary: The partial document provides an overview of microwave frequency bands (K-Band, Ka-Band, Millimeter, and Sub-millimeter) and their characteristic features and applications. It highlights the directive nature of microwaves, their heating effects for applications like cooking and industrial processes, and their use in spectroscopy and pollution control. The document also describes microwave systems, focusing on components like waveguides and transmission lines, emphasizing low losses and efficient signal transmission. Detailed explanations are provided for microwave components such as E-plane Tee, H-plane Tee, and E-H plane Tee, including their construction, functionality, and applications in signal splitting, combining, and power division.

nodes.title: H-plane Tee
nodes.text:
RMT  2022 -2023   2. H-plane  Tee 
A H-plane  Tee junction  is formed  by cutting  a rectangular  slot along  the width  of a 
main  waveguide  and attaching  another  waveguide  the side arm called  the H-arm as  shown  
in figure  2. The port 1 and port 2 of the main  waveguide  are called  collinear  ports  and port 
3 is the  H-arm or  side arm. 
H-plane  Tee is so called  because  the axis of the side arm is parallel  to the planes  of 
the main transmission line. As all three arms of H -plane Tee lie in the plane of magnetic  
field, the magnetic field divides itself into the arms. Therefore this is also called a current  
junction.  
 
 
Figure  2: (a) Schematic  details  and (b) constru ction  of H-plane  Tee Junction  
 
3. E-H plane  Tee 
A H-plane Tee junction is formed by cutting a rectangular slot along the width and  
breadth  of a long waveguide  and side arms  are attached  as shown  in figure  3. Ports  1 and 2 are 
collinear  arms, port 3 is the  H-arm, and port 4 is the E -arm. 
Such a device became necessary because of the difficulty of obtaining a completely  
matched three port Tee junction. This four port hybrid Tee junction combines the power dividing  
properties of both H -plane Tee and E -plane Tee and has the advantage of being completely  
matched  at all its ports.  
Figure  3: E-H Plane  (a) Schematic  (b) Construction  
 
 
4. Directional  Coupler
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the H-plane Tee and E-H plane Tee junctions in waveguides. It explains the construction and functionality of the H-plane Tee, where a rectangular slot is cut along the width of a main waveguide, and a side arm (H-arm) is attached, with all arms lying in the plane of the magnetic field, making it a current junction. The E-H plane Tee is described as a four-port hybrid Tee junction formed by cutting slots along both the width and breadth of a waveguide, with side arms (H-arm and E-arm) attached. This design addresses the challenge of achieving a completely matched three-port Tee junction and combines the power-dividing properties of both H-plane and E-plane Tees, ensuring matched ports.

nodes.title: E-H plane Tee
nodes.text:
RMT  2022 -2023   2. H-plane  Tee 
A H-plane  Tee junction  is formed  by cutting  a rectangular  slot along  the width  of a 
main  waveguide  and attaching  another  waveguide  the side arm called  the H-arm as  shown  
in figure  2. The port 1 and port 2 of the main  waveguide  are called  collinear  ports  and port 
3 is the  H-arm or  side arm. 
H-plane  Tee is so called  because  the axis of the side arm is parallel  to the planes  of 
the main transmission line. As all three arms of H -plane Tee lie in the plane of magnetic  
field, the magnetic field divides itself into the arms. Therefore this is also called a current  
junction.  
 
 
Figure  2: (a) Schematic  details  and (b) constru ction  of H-plane  Tee Junction  
 
3. E-H plane  Tee 
A H-plane Tee junction is formed by cutting a rectangular slot along the width and  
breadth  of a long waveguide  and side arms  are attached  as shown  in figure  3. Ports  1 and 2 are 
collinear  arms, port 3 is the  H-arm, and port 4 is the E -arm. 
Such a device became necessary because of the difficulty of obtaining a completely  
matched three port Tee junction. This four port hybrid Tee junction combines the power dividing  
properties of both H -plane Tee and E -plane Tee and has the advantage of being completely  
matched  at all its ports.  
Figure  3: E-H Plane  (a) Schematic  (b) Construction  
 
 
4. Directional  Coupler  
RMT  2022 -2023   Directional coupler is four port device. Here power can be divided into almost in any ratio i.e. it  
samples energy flow in particular direction. It has a property that wave incident at port 1 coupler  
the power in 2&3 but not at port 4. Similarly power incident at port 4 coupled with 3&2 only met  
with 1, it means port 4&1 are decoupled i.e. isolated port (S14=0). Similarly port 2&3 are also  
decoupled.  It consist  of two wave  guide  with a suitable  couplin g aperture  located  on common  wall 
of wave guide  .The distance  between  two aperture  must be  odd multiple  of λg/4.The  performance  
&characteristics  of directional coupler is determine  by following  parameter.  
 
1) Coupling(C) =  10 log  (pi/pf)  
2) Directivity  (D)=  10 log  (pf/pb)  db 
3) Isolation  (I) =10 log  (pi/pb) db  
4) Insertion  loss (L)=10  log (pi/pt)  db 
Main  line insertion  loss is the attenuation  introduced  in transmission  line by insertion  of 
coupler.  
It is defined  as,Insertion  Loss  = 10 log 10P1/P2  When  power is  entered  at port  1. 
 
 
 
Figure  4: Directional  Coupler  
 
 
5. Isolator  
An Isolator  is a two-port device  that transfers  energy  from  input  to output  with little 
attenuation  and from output to input with very  high attenuation.  
This requirement  is very much  desirable  when  want  to match  a source  with a variable  load.  
In most microwave generators, the output amplitude and frequency tend to fluctuate very  
significantly with changes in load impedance. This is due to mismatch of generator output to  
the load resulting  in reflected  wave  from  load.  But these  reflected  wave  should  not be allowed  
to reach the microwave generator, which will cause amplitude and fre quency instability the  
microwave generator.  
When  the isolator  is inserted  between  generator  and load,  the generator  is coupled  to the load 
with zero attenuation  and reflections  if any from  the load side are completely  absorbed  by the 
isolator without affecting this generator output. Hence, the generator appears to be matched  
for all loads in the presence of  isolator  so that there is no change in frequency and output  
power  due to variation in the  load.
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers the following main points:

1. **H-plane Tee Junction**: Describes the structure and function of an H-plane Tee junction, formed by attaching a side arm (H-arm) to a main waveguide. It explains that the magnetic field divides itself into the arms, making it a current junction.

2. **E-H Plane Tee Junction**: Explains the construction and purpose of an E-H plane Tee junction, a four-port hybrid device combining the properties of H-plane and E-plane Tees. It highlights its advantage of being completely matched at all ports.

3. **Directional Coupler**: Discusses the structure and functionality of a four-port directional coupler, which divides power in specific ratios and isolates certain ports. Key performance parameters such as coupling, directivity, isolation, and insertion loss are defined.

4. **Isolator**: Describes the isolator as a two-port device that allows energy transfer from input to output with minimal attenuation while preventing reflected waves from reaching the source. It emphasizes its role in stabilizing microwave generators by matching the generator to variable loads.

nodes.title: Directional Coupler
nodes.text:
RMT  2022 -2023   Directional coupler is four port device. Here power can be divided into almost in any ratio i.e. it  
samples energy flow in particular direction. It has a property that wave incident at port 1 coupler  
the power in 2&3 but not at port 4. Similarly power incident at port 4 coupled with 3&2 only met  
with 1, it means port 4&1 are decoupled i.e. isolated port (S14=0). Similarly port 2&3 are also  
decoupled.  It consist  of two wave  guide  with a suitable  couplin g aperture  located  on common  wall 
of wave guide  .The distance  between  two aperture  must be  odd multiple  of λg/4.The  performance  
&characteristics  of directional coupler is determine  by following  parameter.  
 
1) Coupling(C) =  10 log  (pi/pf)  
2) Directivity  (D)=  10 log  (pf/pb)  db 
3) Isolation  (I) =10 log  (pi/pb) db  
4) Insertion  loss (L)=10  log (pi/pt)  db 
Main  line insertion  loss is the attenuation  introduced  in transmission  line by insertion  of 
coupler.  
It is defined  as,Insertion  Loss  = 10 log 10P1/P2  When  power is  entered  at port  1. 
 
 
 
Figure  4: Directional  Coupler  
 
 
5. Isolator  
An Isolator  is a two-port device  that transfers  energy  from  input  to output  with little 
attenuation  and from output to input with very  high attenuation.  
This requirement  is very much  desirable  when  want  to match  a source  with a variable  load.  
In most microwave generators, the output amplitude and frequency tend to fluctuate very  
significantly with changes in load impedance. This is due to mismatch of generator output to  
the load resulting  in reflected  wave  from  load.  But these  reflected  wave  should  not be allowed  
to reach the microwave generator, which will cause amplitude and fre quency instability the  
microwave generator.  
When  the isolator  is inserted  between  generator  and load,  the generator  is coupled  to the load 
with zero attenuation  and reflections  if any from  the load side are completely  absorbed  by the 
isolator without affecting this generator output. Hence, the generator appears to be matched  
for all loads in the presence of  isolator  so that there is no change in frequency and output  
power  due to variation in the  load.
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the directional coupler and isolator, both microwave components. It explains the directional coupler as a four-port device used to divide power in specific ratios, with ports 1 and 4, as well as ports 2 and 3, being decoupled. Key parameters for evaluating its performance include coupling, directivity, isolation, and insertion loss, with formulas provided for each. The isolator is described as a two-port device that allows energy transfer from input to output with minimal attenuation while preventing reflected waves from reaching the generator. This ensures stable amplitude and frequency output by matching the generator to variable loads.

nodes.title: Isolator
nodes.text:
RMT  2022 -2023   Directional coupler is four port device. Here power can be divided into almost in any ratio i.e. it  
samples energy flow in particular direction. It has a property that wave incident at port 1 coupler  
the power in 2&3 but not at port 4. Similarly power incident at port 4 coupled with 3&2 only met  
with 1, it means port 4&1 are decoupled i.e. isolated port (S14=0). Similarly port 2&3 are also  
decoupled.  It consist  of two wave  guide  with a suitable  couplin g aperture  located  on common  wall 
of wave guide  .The distance  between  two aperture  must be  odd multiple  of λg/4.The  performance  
&characteristics  of directional coupler is determine  by following  parameter.  
 
1) Coupling(C) =  10 log  (pi/pf)  
2) Directivity  (D)=  10 log  (pf/pb)  db 
3) Isolation  (I) =10 log  (pi/pb) db  
4) Insertion  loss (L)=10  log (pi/pt)  db 
Main  line insertion  loss is the attenuation  introduced  in transmission  line by insertion  of 
coupler.  
It is defined  as,Insertion  Loss  = 10 log 10P1/P2  When  power is  entered  at port  1. 
 
 
 
Figure  4: Directional  Coupler  
 
 
5. Isolator  
An Isolator  is a two-port device  that transfers  energy  from  input  to output  with little 
attenuation  and from output to input with very  high attenuation.  
This requirement  is very much  desirable  when  want  to match  a source  with a variable  load.  
In most microwave generators, the output amplitude and frequency tend to fluctuate very  
significantly with changes in load impedance. This is due to mismatch of generator output to  
the load resulting  in reflected  wave  from  load.  But these  reflected  wave  should  not be allowed  
to reach the microwave generator, which will cause amplitude and fre quency instability the  
microwave generator.  
When  the isolator  is inserted  between  generator  and load,  the generator  is coupled  to the load 
with zero attenuation  and reflections  if any from  the load side are completely  absorbed  by the 
isolator without affecting this generator output. Hence, the generator appears to be matched  
for all loads in the presence of  isolator  so that there is no change in frequency and output  
power  due to variation in the  load.  
RMT  2022 -2023    
 
Figure  5 : Isolator  
 
 
6. Attenuator  
The attenuator  is a two port bidirectional  device  which  attenuates  some  power  when  inserted  
into a transmission line.  
Attenuation  A (dB) =  10 log  (P1/P2)  
Where,  P1 = Power  detected  by the load without  the attenuator  in the line 
P2 = Power detected by  the load with the  attenuator in the line.  
It consists of rectangular waveguide, which gives out the wave with resistive apposition. It is  
made up of Teflon and Polystyrene material .It restricts the power in desired direction. Fixed  
atten uation is defined as the ratio of power absorbed by the load with attenuation in the line.  
Attenuator used as variable attenuator .It is considerably reduced maximum attenuation .It is so  
because  the electric  intensity  there  is much  lower  for dominant  mode  .So they are made  
perpendicular  to electric field place  apart.  
Following  characteristics  of attenuators can be  studied  
1) Input  VSWR  
2) Insertion  loss 
3) Amount  of attenuation  offered  in to the lines  
4) Frequency  sensitivity  
 
7. Circulator  
Circulator is defined as device with ports arranged such that energy entering a port is  
coupled to an adjacent port but not coupled to the other ports. This is depicted in figure  
circulator  can have  any number  of ports.  
 
 
Figure  6 : The symbol  of a circul ator
nodes.summary:
The partial document provides an overview of several microwave components and their characteristics:

1. **Directional Coupler**: A four-port device used to divide power in specific ratios and sample energy flow in a particular direction. Ports 1 and 4, as well as ports 2 and 3, are decoupled. It consists of two waveguides with coupling apertures, and its performance is determined by parameters like coupling, directivity, isolation, and insertion loss.

2. **Isolator**: A two-port device that allows energy transfer from input to output with minimal attenuation while preventing reflected waves from reaching the source. It ensures stable amplitude and frequency of microwave generators by matching the generator to variable loads.

3. **Attenuator**: A two-port bidirectional device that reduces power in a transmission line. It is made of materials like Teflon and Polystyrene and offers fixed or variable attenuation. Key characteristics include input VSWR, insertion loss, attenuation level, and frequency sensitivity.

4. **Circulator**: A multi-port device where energy entering one port is coupled to an adjacent port but not to others. It is used to direct energy flow between ports in a specific sequence.

nodes.summary:
The partial document primarily covers the following main points:

1. **Isolator**: Briefly mentioned as a figure in the document.
2. **Attenuator**: Described as a two-port bidirectional device used to attenuate power in a transmission line. The formula for attenuation is provided, along with details about its construction (rectangular waveguide made of Teflon and Polystyrene) and its function to restrict power in a desired direction. Characteristics such as input VSWR, insertion loss, attenuation, and frequency sensitivity are outlined.
3. **Circulator**: Defined as a multi-port device where energy entering one port is coupled to an adjacent port but not to others. A figure symbolizing the circulator is referenced.
4. **Review Questions**: A set of questions is provided, covering topics such as microwaves, frequency bands, microwave systems, waveguide tees, isolators, circulators, directional couplers, velocity modulation, klystron principles, and attenuators. These questions aim to test understanding of microwave technology concepts.

nodes.text:
RMT  2022 -2023   K- Band  18.0 to 27.0  
Ka-Band  27.0 to 40.0  
Millimeter  40.0 to 300  
Sub-millimeter  300 and above.  
Characteristic  Features of  Microwave  And Applications  
Most  of the applications  of microwave  arise  from  the characteristic  features  and their 
advantages.  First important  characteristic  is that the microwave  is highly  directive  which  makes  
it possible  for microwave  communi cation  used for telephone  networks,  radio  broadcasting  and 
television systems. Like any other energy, microwave energy has a heating effect, it is used in  
microwave oven for home cooking, drying machine, drying inks, and in food processing  
industries.  
Microwaves are capable of energetically interacting with matter and so used in microwave  
spectroscopy  for structural  analysis.  Apart  from  scientific  research  the absorption  of microwave  
by molecular resonance is well suited for various industrial measureme nts like control of  
pollution  by checking  the concentration  of different  gases  from an exhaust chimney.  
Microwave  Systems:  
Microwave system normally consists of several microwave components including the source  
and the load being connected to each other by  waveguide or co -axial or transmission line  
system.  All these  components  must  be built with low standing  wave  ratios,  low attenuator,  lower  
insertion  losses  and character  to achieve  the desired  transmission  of microwave  signal.  
Following  are the different microwave  components.  
1. E-plane  Tee 
A   rectangular slot is cut along the broader dimension of a long waveguide and a  
side arm is attached as shown in fig. Port 1 and Port 2 are the collinear arms and Port 3 is the  
E-arm. 
When  TE10 mode  is made  to propagate  into Port 3 the two outputs  at Port 1 and 2 will have 
a phase shift of 1800.Since the electric field lines change their direction when they come out  
of Port 1 and Port 2, it is called an E -plane Tee. E -plane Tee is a voltage or series junction  
symmetrical  about  the central  arm. Hence  any signals  that is to be split or any two signals  that 
are to be  combined will be  fed from the E  arm. 
 
 
 
 
 
Figure  1: (a) Schematic  of E-plane  Tee (b) 
Construction  (c) Electric field  lines with  180°  
phase  shift
nodes.summary: The partial document provides an overview of microwave frequency bands (K-Band, Ka-Band, Millimeter, and Sub-millimeter) and their characteristic features and applications. It highlights the directive nature of microwaves, enabling their use in communication systems like telephone networks, radio broadcasting, and television. The heating effect of microwaves is discussed, with applications in microwave ovens, drying machines, and food processing industries. The document also mentions the use of microwaves in spectroscopy for structural analysis and industrial measurements, such as pollution control. Additionally, it describes the components of a microwave system, emphasizing the importance of low standing wave ratios, low attenuation, and efficient transmission. The E-plane Tee is introduced as a microwave component, explaining its structure, functionality, and phase-shifting properties.

nodes.text:
RMT  2022 -2023    
 
D.Y.  Patil  College  of Engineering,  Akurdi,  Pune  – 44 
 
Department  of Electronics  and Telecommunication  Engineering  
 
 
Name  of student:  Anjali Kumari  PRN  No.:72017900K  
Class  & Division:  BE-B Roll No.: BEB224  
 
 
Experiment  No.: 
 
Date  of Performance:  
 
AIM:  To study  the different microwave  components.  
 
APPARATUS:  E-plane  Tee, H-plane  Tee, E-H plane  Tee, Directional  coupler,  Isolator,  
Attenuator  and Circulator.  
 
THEORY:  
 
Introduction  To Microwave  Communications  
Microwaves are electromagnetic waves (E.M. waves) having wavelength in the micron range.  
Though microwave frequencies refer to those from 1GHz to 106GHz but generally used for those  
wavelengths measured in centimeters, roughly from 10cm to 1cm(3 to 30 GHz)  and the waves  
having  wavelengths  less than 1cm corresponds  to higher  frequencies(>30  GHz)  are called  
millimeter  waves (mm waves).  
Microwave  Frequencies  
Relationship  between  the frequency  (f) and the wavelength  ( λ) of an E.M. wave  is λ 
f=c 
Where  c is velocity  of electromagnetic  radiation,  usually  called the speed  of light.  
IEEE  Microwave  Frequency  Band:  
Designation  Frequency  range  in GHz  
HF 0.003 to 0.03  
VHF  0.03 to 0.3  
UHF  0.3 to 1.0  
L-Band  1.0 to 2.0  
S-Band  2.0 to 4.0  
C-Band  4.0 to 8.0  
X-Band  8.0to 12.0  
Ku-Band  12.0 to 27.0
nodes.summary: The partial document is a laboratory experiment record from the Department of Electronics and Telecommunication Engineering at D.Y. Patil College of Engineering, Akurdi, Pune. It includes details such as the student's name, PRN, class, and roll number. The experiment aims to study different microwave components, with apparatus including E-plane Tee, H-plane Tee, E-H plane Tee, directional coupler, isolator, attenuator, and circulator. The theory section introduces microwave communications, explaining electromagnetic waves in the microwave frequency range (1 GHz to 106 GHz) and their relationship between frequency and wavelength. It also provides the IEEE microwave frequency band designations and their respective frequency ranges.

The main points covered include the definition of microwaves, frequency bands for millimeter waves, IEEE microwave frequency bands, characteristics and applications of microwave technology, components of a microwave system, waveguide tees and their types, isolators, circulators, directional couplers, velocity modulation, principles of Klystron operation, conditions for maximum output power in reflex klystrons, and the concept of attenuators.

It includes details about a student, Anjali Kumari, and outlines an experiment aimed at studying different microwave components. The document lists the apparatus required, such as E-plane Tee, H-plane Tee, E-H plane Tee, directional coupler, isolator, attenuator, and circulator.
